Abbreviations: RSV = respiratory syncytial virus; RSVpreF = maternal RSV vaccine.
* Infants born <14 days after maternal vaccination should receive RSV monoclonal antibody. RSV monoclonal antibody may also be considered for infants born to vaccinated mothers in rare circumstances when, based on the clinical judgment of the health care provider, the potential incremental benefit of administration is warranted. These situations include but are not limited to 1) infants born to mothers who might not mount an adequate immune response to vaccination (e.g., mothers with immunocompromising conditions); 2) infants born to mothers who have conditions associated with reduced transplacental antibody transfer (e.g., mothers living with HIV infection); 3) infants who undergo procedures leading to loss of maternal antibodies (e.g., cardiopulmonary bypass, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation, or exchange transfusion); and 4) infants at substantially increased risk for severe RSV disease (e.g., hemodynamically significant congenital heart disease or intensive care unit admission with oxygen requirement at discharge).
